clusters.title: Experience: I sat under an oak tree every day for a year
clusters.neutral_headline: Experience: I sat under an oak tree every day for a year

items.title: Experience: I sat under an oak tree every day for a year
items.standfirst: After a period of burnout, I realised that nature knows what you need, and is always ready to offer it – you just have to be quiet enough to receive itIn 2022 I moved to Clevedon, near Bristol. As soon as I saw the oak tree behind my flat, I started sitting under it. It’s not in some beautiful, remote place – it’s on an urban hill surrounded by grassland – but as a solitary tree on the side of a hill, it drew my attention. I was burned out. For 10 years, I had run a nonprofit tackling plastic pollution. We had got the government to ban plastic cutlery and polystyrene takeaway packaging, and supermarkets to ban plastic cotton buds. They were major achievements, but it was hard work and I was exhausted. I was transitioning away from activism, and only working three days a week. Continue reading...
items.content: After a period of burnout, I realised that nature knows what you need, and is always ready to offer it – you just have to be quiet enough to receive itIn 2022 I moved to Clevedon, near Bristol. For 10 years, I had run a nonprofit tackling plastic pollution. We had got the government to ban plastic cutlery and polystyrene takeaway packaging, and supermarkets to ban plastic cotton buds. They were major achievements, but it was hard work and I was exhausted. I was transitioning away from activism, and only working three days a week. Continue reading...

clusters.title: Girl, 5, traumatised after GP assistant wrongly prescribed vaginal pessary, report finds
clusters.neutral_headline: Girl, 5, traumatised after GP assistant wrongly prescribed...

items.title: Girl, 5, traumatised after GP assistant wrongly prescribed vaginal pessary, report finds
items.standfirst: Mother, who thought daughter was being examined by GP, says girl began to bleed and scream in pain after device was insertedA five-year-old was left traumatised, bleeding and in severe pain after a physician associate wrongly prescribed her a vaginal pessary, according to a damning report by the health ombudsman.The parliamentary and health service ombudsman said there were “multiple failures” in the care of the girl, who saw a physician associate (PA) at a GP practice in the East Midlands after complaining of itching and vaginal discharge. Continue reading...
items.content: Mother, who thought daughter was being examined by GP, says girl began to bleed and scream in pain after device was insertedA five-year-old was left traumatised, bleeding and in severe pain after a physician associate wrongly prescribed her a vaginal pessary, according to a damning report by the health ombudsman.The parliamentary and health service ombudsman said there were “multiple failures” in the care of the girl, who saw a physician associate (PA) at a GP practice in the East Midlands after complaining of itching and vaginal discharge. clusters.title: Family of girl left brain-damaged at birth accept £28m NHS payout
clusters.neutral_headline: Family of girl left brain-damaged at birth accept £28m NHS payout

items.title: Family of girl left brain-damaged at birth accept £28m NHS payout
items.standfirst: Mother demands overhaul of maternity care after settling case over birth at Queen’s hospital in Romford in 2019The family of a girl left brain-damaged at birth have agreed to accept £28m in damages after the NHS trust involved admitted that its mistakes led to the tragedy.Barking, Havering and Redbridge university hospitals NHS trust failed to monitor the baby’s heart rate while her mother was in labour or ask an obstetrician to review the case, either of which might have led to the girl being born in a healthy condition. Continue reading...
items.content: Mother demands overhaul of maternity care after settling case over birth at Queen’s hospital in Romford in 2019The family of a girl left brain-damaged at birth have agreed to accept £28m in damages after the NHS trust involved admitted that its mistakes led to the tragedy.Barking, Havering and Redbridge university hospitals NHS trust failed to monitor the baby’s heart rate while her mother was in labour or ask an obstetrician to review the case, either of which might have led to the girl being born in a healthy condition. clusters.title: The Guardian view on NHS records: patients are not raw material for big tech | Editorial
clusters.neutral_headline: The Guardian view on NHS records: patients are not raw material...

items.title: The Guardian view on NHS records: patients are not raw material for big tech | Editorial
items.standfirst: Ministers should end Palantir’s contract before medical confidentiality is sacrificed to Silicon Valley’s appetite for public dataAlarm bells ought to have rung when it emerged last month that Palantir engineers could gain “unlimited access” to identifiable NHS patient data. Such sensitive medical information was only supposed to be available either to someone involved in a patient’s care or with the patient’s informed consent. NHS England’s new position appears to have changed that, extending access to private companies because it may make data processing easier. Convenience is not a basis for undermining medical confidentiality.Nicola Byrne, the government’s national data guardian, clearly thought the NHS had broken its promise that its £330m deal with Palantir would see “identifiable patient information … limited to NHS staff with a legitimate need”. Patients tell doctors things they may tell no one else. If they think that sensitive details can be disclosed to US tech corporations, trust will suffer – and patients will say less when the truth matters most.Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in our letters section, please click here. Continue reading...
items.content: Ministers should end Palantir’s contract before medical confidentiality is sacrificed to Silicon Valley’s appetite for public dataAlarm bells ought to have rung when it emerged last month that Palantir engineers could gain “unlimited access” to identifiable NHS patient data. NHS England’s new position appears to have changed that, extending access to private companies because it may make data processing easier. Convenience is not a basis for undermining medical confidentiality.Nicola Byrne, the government’s national data guardian, clearly thought the NHS had broken its promise that its £330m deal with Palantir would see “identifiable patient information … limited to NHS staff with a legitimate need”. Patients tell doctors things they may tell no one else. clusters.title: Why is Andy Burnham talking about fixing England’s social care system?
clusters.neutral_headline: Why is Andy Burnham talking about fixing England’s social care system
bullet_summary: Prospective MP and potential Labour leader has said tackling the issue is a priority

items.title: Why is Andy Burnham talking about fixing England’s social care system?
items.standfirst: Prospective MP and potential Labour leader has said tackling the issue is a priority. But why is the system in crisis and what can be done about it?‘I wouldn’t flinch’: Burnham on social care, markets, Brexit – and the prospect of a general electionAndy Burnham has signalled he would overhaul England’s social care system this year if he became prime minister, the biggest indication yet of what his main priorities would be.Work is already under way on updating the social care system under Keir Starmer, but Burnham has suggested he would seek more radical and urgent change. Continue reading...

clusters.title: Reassurance for bladder cancer patients | Letters
clusters.neutral_headline: Reassurance for bladder cancer patients | Letters

items.title: Reassurance for bladder cancer patients | Letters
items.standfirst: Gail Cartmail offers a positive outlook to those facing life-changing bladder surgery, based on her own experienceThe report of treatment being trialled that could potentially spare bladder cancer patients life-changing surgery is welcome news (Doctors hail drug that spares bladder cancer patients ‘life-changing’ surgery, 2 June). Yet readers currently facing surgery that includes removing their bladder are likely to be concerned about the here and now.Like Tracey Emin, I was diagnosed with bladder cancer in 2020. Life requires some planning. For example, the paucity of public toilets means mapping alternatives, as bladder bags have much less capacity than a natural bladder. I always carry a spare kit, however; following the advice and guidance of stoma nurse specialists, it is possible to avoid leaks. The Urostomy Association is a mine of useful information. Continue reading...

Yet readers currently facing surgery that includes removing their bladder are likely to be concerned about the here and now.Like Tracey Emin, I was diagnosed with bladder cancer in 2020. clusters.title: Three studies used by RFK Jr and allies to justify controversial vaccine policy changes facing new scrutiny
clusters.neutral_headline: Three studies used by RFK Jr and allies to justify controversial...

items.title: Three studies used by RFK Jr and allies to justify controversial vaccine policy changes facing new scrutiny
items.standfirst: Scientists praise moves to investigate, retract or remove controversial studies. The authors stand by their workThree scientific papers that raised questions about vaccine safety and were used by the Trump administration to justify controversial changes to US vaccine policies have over the last two months been removed, retracted or placed under investigation by the journals that published them.In some cases, the actions occurred years after scientists first raised alarms about the studies’ scientific merits. Continue reading...

clusters.title: 'I've lost my butt': how rapid weight loss can leave you with less muscle and more fat
clusters.neutral_headline: 'I've lost my butt': how rapid weight loss can leave you...

items.title: 'I've lost my butt': how rapid weight loss can leave you with less muscle and more fat
items.standfirst: GLP-1 drugs such as Mounjaro are helping millions of people rapidly lose weight. But the changes happening inside the body go far beyond the number on the scale.Neelam Tailor investigates the growing debate around the possible risks of rapid weight loss from jabs and yo-yo dieting, which include loss of lean mass and consequences in older age. Experts say the debate isn’t just about weight-loss drugs, but about how modern dieting culture has shaped our bodies for decades Continue reading...

Experts say the debate isn’t just about weight-loss drugs, but about how modern dieting culture has shaped our bodies for decades Continue reading...

clusters.title: The race to combat Ebola: what vaccines and treatments are being developed and how long will it take?
clusters.neutral_headline: The race to combat Ebola: what vaccines and treatments are being developed...

items.title: The race to combat Ebola: what vaccines and treatments are being developed and how long will it take?
items.standfirst: With the Bundibugyo strain of the disease spreading across the DRC and Uganda, scientists and researchers are trying to find rapid solutionsThere is no vaccine or treatment available for the Bundibugyo strain of Ebola that is spreading in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) and Uganda, but this week three vaccine developers were awarded $60m (£45m) in emergency funding as the race to halt the outbreak ramps up.Security issues in the affected region of the DRC, where conflict has displaced tens of thousands, have made it challenging to set up trials to test drugs. Militias operate in the area and some Ebola treatment centres have been attacked. Continue reading...
